cards.answer: Ratio of alveolar ventilation to pulmonary blood flow. Normal overall V/Q = 0.8. At lung apex: high V/Q (more ventilation than perfusion). At base: low V/Q (more perfusion than ventilation). V/Q mismatch is the most common cause of hypoxaemia.

tip.tip: Gravity affects both ventilation and perfusion.

cards.text: What is surfactant and why is it important?
cards.answer: Produced by type II alveolar cells. Main component: dipalmitoyl phosphatidylcholine (DPPC). Reduces surface tension, prevents alveolar collapse (atelectasis), increases compliance. Deficiency in premature infants causes respiratory distress syndrome (RDS).

tip.tip: Without it, small alveoli would collapse into large ones.

cards.answer: Increased CO2 and H+ (lower pH) decrease haemoglobin's affinity for O2 (rightward shift of O2-Hb curve). This promotes O2 unloading in metabolically active tissues. The reverse occurs in lungs (Haldane effect: O2 binding promotes CO2 release).

tip.tip: Named after Christian Bohr, helps tissues get more O2.

cards.text: What are the lung volumes and capacities?
cards.answer: Volumes: tidal (500mL), inspiratory reserve (3000mL), expiratory reserve (1100mL), residual (1200mL). Capacities: vital capacity (TV+IRV+ERV=4600mL), total lung capacity (VC+RV=5800mL), functional residual capacity (ERV+RV=2300mL), inspiratory capacity (TV+IRV).

tip.tip: Volumes are measured, capacities are combinations.

cards.text: How does the chloride shift work?
cards.answer: In tissues: CO2 enters RBC -> carbonic anhydrase converts to H2CO3 -> dissociates to H+ and HCO3-. HCO3- moves out to plasma, Cl- moves in (chloride shift) to maintain electrical neutrality. Reversed in lungs. H+ buffered by deoxyhaemoglobin.

tip.tip: Maintains electrical neutrality across RBC membrane.

cards.text: What is hypoxia and what are its types?
cards.answer: Hypoxic: low PaO2 (altitude, lung disease). Anaemic: low Hb (anaemia, CO poisoning). Stagnant/circulatory: reduced blood flow (shock, heart failure). Histotoxic: cells cannot use O2 (cyanide poisoning). Only hypoxic hypoxia responds to supplemental O2.

tip.tip: Four types based on where the problem lies.

cards.text: What is compliance and how is it measured?
cards.answer: Change in lung volume per unit change in transpulmonary pressure. High compliance = easily distensible (emphysema). Low compliance = stiff lungs (fibrosis, pulmonary oedema, ARDS). Measured from pressure-volume curve. Surfactant increases compliance.

cards.text: What is the work of breathing?
cards.answer: Elastic work: overcoming elastic recoil of lungs and chest wall (65%). Resistive work: overcoming airway resistance (35%). Increased in: asthma (resistive), fibrosis (elastic), obesity (chest wall). Quiet breathing uses only inspiratory muscles; expiration is passive.

tip.tip: Quiet expiration is passive - no muscular effort needed.
